title: Association Between Acute Gastrointestinal Injury and Mortality Risk in Critically Ill Patients: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
authors: Tianye Gao, MD + Jie Liu, PhD et al.
one_line_summary: Severe acute gastrointestinal injury (AGI grades III-IV or GIDS 2-4) is strongly associated with nearly three-fold increased **mortality** risk in **critically** ill ICU patients.
key_pearls: Severe GI dysfunction (AGI III-IV or GIDS 2-4) is associated with a pooled OR of **2.78** (95% CI **2.19-3.52**) for short-term **mortality** in ICU patients.
Both the AGI grading system (OR **2.75**) and the GIDS (OR **3.18**) provide comparable prognostic information with no **significant** difference between tools (P=0.74).
The **mortality** association is consistent across 28-day/ICU **mortality** (OR **2.70**) and in-hospital **mortality** (OR **4.27**), with no statistical subgroup difference (P=0.37).
Sensitivity analysis restricted to 28-day/ICU **mortality** outcomes yielded an OR of **2.70** (95% CI **2.04-3.58**) with *I²*=0%, confirming robust association.
Only 2 of 8 studies provided adjusted effect estimates; the pooled unadjusted OR from 6 studies with raw count data was **3.46** (95% CI **2.76-4.33**).
Early recognition and systematic assessment of GI dysfunction using standardized grading systems may facilitate risk stratification and guide clinical management.
The majority of included studies were conducted in China, limiting generalizability; validation in diverse non-Asian populations is needed.
population: Adult ICU patients (mean age 52.3-68.7 years, male 53.1-71.3%) with AGI grades III-IV or GIDS 2-4 vs mild/no GI dysfunction

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content:
- GI dysfunction prevalence in ICU patients ranges from **30% to 62%**.
- The GI tract maintains homeostasis through digestive, absorptive, immune, endocrine, and barrier functions; disruption triggers bacterial translocation, inflammatory mediator release, and progression to *multiple organ dysfunction syndrome*.
- In 2012, the *ESICM-WGAP* introduced a standardized 4-grade classification for AGI: Grade I (self-limiting), Grade II (requires intervention), Grade III (GI failure not restorable), Grade IV (immediately life-threatening GI failure).
- The *GIDS* (0-4 point scale) was developed as a simplified numerical bedside tool and validated in the international multicenter *iSOFA study*.
- Previous meta-analyses examined overall AGI prevalence but lacked focused dose-response assessment between severity grades and **mortality**; comparative prognostic value of AGI vs GIDS had not been systematically evaluated.
- Study objectives: (i) quantitatively synthesize association between severe AGI (grades III-IV or GIDS 2-4) and short-term **mortality**, (ii) examine consistency across outcome definitions and assessment tools, (iii) evaluate evidence quality and identify research gaps.

sections.heading: Methods
sections.content:
- Conducted per *PRISMA 2020* statement with predefined methods.
- **Eligibility**: Adult ICU patients (≥18 years); exposure defined as AGI grades III-IV (vs 0-II) using *ESICM-WGAP 2012* criteria or GIDS 2-4 (vs 0-1); primary outcome was short-term all-cause **mortality** (prioritizing 28-day or ICU **mortality**).
- **Excluded**: cardiac surgery patients (AGI related to cardiopulmonary bypass), COVID-19 populations (distinct pathophysiology, prone positioning confounds), non-ICU populations, studies without AGI/GIDS definitions, intervention trials where AGI was the target, case series/reviews/abstracts, duplicate cohorts, ECMO populations.
- **Search**: MEDLINE, Embase, Web of Science, Cochrane Central, CNKI, Wanfang from **January 1, 2016 to January 31, 2025**; no language restrictions.
- **Data extraction**: Two independent reviewers; collected first author, year, country, design, ICU type, sample size, baseline characteristics (age, sex, APACHE II, SOFA), AGI/GIDS assessment windows, outcome definitions, 2x2 contingency data, adjusted ORs/HRs with covariates.
- **Risk of bias**: *Newcastle-Ottawa Scale* (NOS); high quality 7-9, moderate 4-6, low 0-3.
- **Statistical analysis**: Random-effects model with *DerSimonian-Laird* method; heterogeneity via *I²* (25% low, 50% moderate, 75% high) and Q test; prespecified subgroup analyses by scoring system type and outcome type; sensitivity analyses (AGI-only, 28-day/ICU **mortality** only, count data only, leave-one-out); publication bias via funnel plot and *Egger regression test*.

sections.heading: Results
sections.content:
- **Study selection**: 622 records identified; 451 after deduplication; 67 full-texts assessed; **8 studies** included (6 with 2x2 count data, 2 with adjusted estimates only).
- **Study characteristics**: Published 2016-2024; 6 studies from China, 1 multicenter European; 6 used AGI grading, 2 used GIDS; 6 prospective, 2 retrospective cohorts; sample sizes ranged from 90 to 1,102; total **2,786 patients**.
- Mean age range **52.3-68.7 years**; male proportion **53.1-71.3%**; APACHE II scores **15.2-24.8**; SOFA scores **5.8-10.4**.
- **Quality**: 6 studies scored NOS 8-9 (high quality); 2 studies scored 6 (moderate quality).
- **Primary meta-analysis**: Pooled OR **2.78** (95% CI **2.19-3.52**, P<0.001); moderate heterogeneity *I²*=**42.5%** (P=0.09).
- Individual study ORs ranged from **1.94** (Shen et al) to **4.27** (Hai et al); 7 of 8 studies showed statistically **significant** associations.
- **Subgroup by outcome**: 28-day/ICU **mortality** (n=5) pooled OR **2.70** (95% CI **2.04-3.58**, *I²*=0%); in-hospital **mortality** (n=1) OR **4.27** (95% CI **1.63-11.18**); test for subgroup differences P=0.37.
- **Subgroup by scoring system**: AGI (n=5) pooled OR **2.75** (95% CI **2.07-3.67**, *I²*=0%); GIDS (n=1) OR **3.18** (95% CI **1.43-7.07**); test for subgroup differences P=0.74.
- **Sensitivity analyses**: AGI-only OR **2.75** (95% CI **2.07-3.67**); 28-day/ICU **mortality** only OR **2.70** (95% CI **2.04-3.58**); 6 studies with 2x2 count data unadjusted OR **3.46** (95% CI **2.76-4.33**, *I²*=0%); leave-one-out ORs ranged **2.58-3.01**.
- **Publication bias**: Funnel plot reasonably symmetric; *Egger test* limited by small study number (k=6).

sections.heading: Discussion
sections.content:
- Severe AGI is strongly associated with nearly **3-fold increased mortality** (OR **2.78**), robust across multiple sensitivity and subgroup analyses.
- Addition of *Reintam Blaser et al (iSOFA study)* (n=540, European multicenter) and *Wang et al* (n=1,102) strengthened evidence, broadened geographic diversity, and increased total sample from 1,144 to **2,786**.
- Both AGI grading and GIDS demonstrate comparable prognostic value (subgroup difference P=0.74), supporting clinician choice based on local expertise and resources.
- The association persisted across 28-day, ICU, and in-hospital **mortality**, suggesting AGI impacts both immediate **survival** and recovery trajectories.
- Moderate heterogeneity (*I²*=**42.5%**) reflects methodological variability (adjusted vs unadjusted estimates), geographic expansion (China to Europe), and scoring system differences; all individual estimates remained in the same direction.
- **Clinical implications**: (i) supports systematic GI function assessment in routine ICU monitoring; (ii) AGI/GIDS could integrate into multidimensional prognostic models alongside APACHE II/SOFA; (iii) potential utility for clinical trial risk stratification and family discussions; (iv) emphasizes evidence-based GI complication management including enteral nutrition optimization, prokinetic use, and intra-abdominal hypertension monitoring.
- **Research gaps**: (i) mechanisms require elucidation via biomarkers of gut barrier function and intestinal perfusion; (ii) interventional trials needed to determine if preventing/reversing GI dysfunction improves outcomes; (iii) optimal timing and frequency of GI assessment and dynamic change monitoring; (iv) validation in non-Asian diverse ICU populations.
- **Limitations**: (i) only 8 studies included, limiting publication bias detection power; (ii) majority conducted in China, limiting generalizability; (iii) only 2 studies provided adjusted estimates, so pooled OR may be confounded by unmeasured illness severity (SOFA/APACHE, vasopressors, mechanical ventilation, renal replacement therapy); (iv) potential exposure misclassification as sicker patients more likely graded AGI III-IV; (v) AGI/GIDS assessment involves clinical judgement introducing measurement variability; (vi) dichotomized exposure definition obscures dose-response relationships; (vii) observational design precludes causal inference.

strengths_limitations:
- **Strengths**: Comprehensive systematic search across multiple databases with no language restrictions; inclusion of 2 large-scale studies (European multicenter iSOFA and Chinese development cohort) substantially increased sample size to 2,786 and broadened geographic diversity; consistent findings across multiple sensitivity and subgroup analyses with low heterogeneity in restricted analyses (*I²*=0%); use of standardized NOS quality assessment.
- **Limitations**: Only 8 studies included, limiting statistical power for publication bias assessment; majority of studies from China, limiting generalizability to non-Asian populations; only 2 studies provided multivariable-adjusted estimates, so pooled OR may be confounded by unmeasured illness severity; potential exposure misclassification and subjective AGI/GIDS assessment; dichotomized exposure obscures dose-response relationships; observational design precludes causal inference.
